title: Style-pack schema, pinning, anima assets, and palette rule
short_description: Implement the full StylePack schema (palette roles, font roles, stroke profile, textures, transition defaults, asset manifest, constraint profile) with per-project version pinning, the anima assets --json vocabulary-discovery command, and the STYLE.PALETTE_VIOLATION verify rule.
long_description_md:
# Style-pack schema, pinning, `anima assets`, and palette verification

## Why this task exists

Style packs are the boundary between anima-ai's general scene-graph engine and its recognizable visual identities. Core rendering must remain genre-neutral; palette, typography, wobble behavior, textures, transitions, curated assets, and taste thresholds belong in versioned data packs rather than renderer subclasses or scattered constants. This is what allows `crude` to establish the Phase 3 paint aesthetic, `flat` to prove pack pluggability in Phase 5, and future third-party packs to extend the system without forking the renderer.

The versioned boundary is also part of the determinism contract. Font changes alter text metrics, palette changes alter output and contrast calculations, and stroke or texture changes alter pixels. Therefore identical project source, seed, engine version, and style-pack version must resolve to the same immutable pack and produce the same graph, verification report, and frames. A project must never silently follow a floating `latest` pack.

This task implements the shared style-pack system required by **Phase 3: Paint rendering + `crude` style pack**. It does not by itself supply the complete `crude` content library or procedural paint renderer; it supplies the schema, exact-version resolver, asset-discovery surface, and first pack-aware verification rule those tasks consume.

## Dependencies and integration points

- `scene-graph-model` supplies `Project.style_pack`, the exact `StylePackRef`, node style references, text font roles, and the public versioned graph codec. Any style-value extension required to distinguish a palette-role reference from an explicit raw color must follow that codec's compatibility policy and remain typed rather than relying on string-shape guessing.
- `font-bundling` supplies the package-resident font manifest and binaries. Style-pack font roles must resolve to those entries through `importlib.resources`; system-font lookup is forbidden.
- `verification-report-schema` supplies `Finding`, `VerificationReport`, the stable finding-code registry, rule versions, deterministic ordering, and report-level `pack_version` stamping.
- `cli-framework` supplies global `--json`, `--project`, typed exits, stdout/stderr separation, the shared error boundary, and human output derived from the same structured command result.

## StylePack public contract

Implement an immutable, typed `StylePack` model and a public, versioned JSON representation. Pack `schema_version` is the format version and is independent of the pack's own content `version`. The root contract contains:

- `id` and an exact `version`; empty versions, ranges, wildcards, aliases such as `latest`, and implicit versions are invalid.
- `palette`: unique agent-readable role names mapped to typed sRGB colors with one canonical serialized representation. Authoring conveniences may accept supported shorthand, but canonical JSON must normalize it deterministically.
- `fonts`: unique role names mapped to bundled font-manifest entries, including the data needed to resolve the family, face, weight, style, and package resource. Every reference must exist in the vetted font manifest, and no absolute or system-font path is permitted.
- `stroke_profile`: finite, validated parameters for wobble amplitude and frequency, width variation, jitter, and any shared stroke defaults required by the paint renderer. Values must have documented units, deterministic defaults, and meaningful non-negative or bounded ranges.
- `textures`: named, ordered texture descriptors referencing package resources or a closed set of engine-provided procedural texture identifiers. Descriptors include the deterministic rendering parameters needed by the paint renderer, such as scale, opacity, and supported blend behavior; resource paths must remain inside the pack and exist at load time.
- `transition_defaults`: named defaults for the transition contexts exposed by the animation system, referencing stable transition/easing identifiers and validated duration/parameter values rather than executable hooks.
- `assets`: a manifest of character and prop entries. Every entry has a unique pack-local ID, type, tags, package-relative resource references, and license metadata. Character entries expose unique pose IDs mapped to resources; prop entries expose their default resource and may expose variants where the schema permits them. Missing resources, duplicate IDs or poses, path traversal, and incomplete license metadata are validation errors.
- `constraint_profile`: deterministic defaults for pack-aware verification rules, keyed by stable namespaced finding code. Each rule declares enabled state, default severity, and registry-validated parameters. The initial profile must configure `STYLE.PALETTE_VIOLATION`; the schema must support later pacing and continuity rules without accepting unvalidated executable code or arbitrary Python objects.

The codec must use JSON primitives only, emit defaulted public fields, reject unknown required versions and malformed content with typed errors, and serialize canonically with deterministic object keys and semantic collection order. A checked-in representative fixture must exercise every field, both asset types, multiple character poses, font and texture resources, transition defaults, and constraint settings.

Packs are data and package resources, never Python renderer subclasses. Loading and validation must perform no network I/O and must not execute pack-provided code.

## Exact project pinning and resolution

The existing project-level `StylePackRef(id, version)` becomes mandatory at build time. A registry/resolver may contain multiple versions of the same pack ID, but resolution is always by the exact `(id, version)` pair. It must never select the newest version, fall back to another version, or rewrite the project pin. Duplicate registrations for the same pair are errors, as is a manifest whose declared identity disagrees with the requested pair.

The resolved pack is immutable for the lifetime of a built project and is the single object consumed by rendering, verification, and asset discovery. Its exact version must populate `VerificationReport.pack_version` and the existing artifact metadata path. Missing packs, unavailable versions, invalid manifests, missing resources, and font-resolution failures must become structured, actionable failures through the existing build/CLI boundaries rather than raw tracebacks. Upgrading a project remains an explicit source change followed by normal verification and golden regeneration; this task does not add an automatic upgrade command.

## `anima assets` vocabulary discovery

Implement `anima assets` on the shared CLI framework. It resolves the active project's exact pinned pack and derives both human and JSON output from the same inventory object. `anima assets --json` must return deterministic, machine-readable vocabulary containing:

- resolved pack ID, content version, and schema version;
- palette role names and canonical resolved colors;
- font roles and stable face metadata;
- named textures and transition defaults;
- character and prop IDs, types, tags, available pose or variant IDs, and license tags.

The command must read the real resolved manifest rather than maintain a second hard-coded catalog. Output must not expose machine-specific absolute resource paths. Ordering must be documented and stable so agents and snapshots can depend on it. A missing project, missing pinned version, or malformed pack returns the CLI's typed user-error result with one JSON document on stdout in JSON mode, diagnostics on stderr, and no traceback. Human table output is formatted from the same inventory data.

Territory and map-dataset discovery joins this command in Phase 4; this task must leave a clean inventory-extension point but must not invent territory data or require the map engine.

## `STYLE.PALETTE_VIOLATION`

Register and activate `STYLE.PALETTE_VIOLATION` as a versioned pack-aware verification rule. The rule walks every palette-bearing value in the built scene graph through one shared style visitor, including nested groups and subtype-specific fill, stroke, text, or override fields. It emits a finding whenever authored scene content uses an explicit raw color where a named pack palette role is required. A raw value remains a violation even when it happens to equal a palette color, because role references—not coincidental hex equality—are what make a project survive pack changes.

Unknown role names raise `REF.UNKNOWN_STYLE_ROLE` — this task OWNS that structural check (namespace defined here, so check ships here; `verify-structural-checks` explicitly excludes it to keep Phase 2 free of Phase 3 dependencies) — and must not produce duplicate palette findings. Each palette finding identifies the node and offending property, reports the allowed role namespace in `expected`, reports the explicit value in `actual`, and supplies a concrete `fix_hint`. If an identical palette color exists, the hint names that role; otherwise it deterministically suggests the nearest applicable role. Findings are emitted in stable graph/property order and carry the rule version and severity from the resolved pack's constraint profile. The initial `crude` profile may treat this taste rule as a warning; pack-level error severity must also work. Per-project promote/demote configuration remains Phase 5 scope.

The rule must participate in the normal `anima verify --json` path and therefore use the existing report object, code registry, pack-version stamp, pass calculation, and human formatter rather than introducing another output shape.

## Explicitly out of scope

- Procedural wobble rasterization, paint/paper texture rendering, paint-reveal animation, or glyph-outline stroke rendering. This task defines their pack data contract; sibling Phase 3 renderer tasks implement the pixels.
- The complete `crude` content bar of at least 12 characters with three poses each, at least 20 props, blob nations, label-pop animations, license vetting, and per-asset golden images. Those assets populate this manifest in a separate Phase 3 task.
- The `flat` pack and its complete content, which ship in Phase 5 against the same schema.
- Territory discovery, map datasets, or map assets, which ship in Phase 4.
- Phase 5 pacing and continuity rules or per-project rule-severity overrides. The constraint schema must accommodate them, but this task implements only the palette rule and pack-level defaults.
- Network installation, a style-pack marketplace, remote manifests, automatic pack upgrades, or executable third-party pack hooks.

deliverables.description: Implement the immutable, typed, schema-versioned StylePack contract covering exact pack identity, palette roles, bundled-font roles, validated stroke profile, texture descriptors, transition defaults, character/prop asset manifest with poses and license metadata, and registry-validated constraint profiles. Include canonical deterministic JSON serialization, strict malformed/unknown-version handling, package-resource and font-manifest validation, and a representative full-contract snapshot fixture.

Implement the offline style-pack registry and exact project-pin resolver. Projects must require an explicit `(pack id, version)` pair; multiple installed versions may coexist, but floating versions, implicit latest selection, fallback to another version, duplicate registrations, identity mismatches, and missing resources must fail deterministically. The resolved version must flow into verification and artifact metadata without mutating the project pin.

Implement `anima assets` with human and `--json` output generated from one deterministic inventory of the active project's exactly pinned pack: pack metadata, palette roles, bundled-font roles, textures, transition defaults, and real character/prop IDs with poses, tags, and license tags. Cover stdout/stderr separation, stable ordering, `--project`, and typed JSON failures for missing projects, versions, and malformed packs.

Register and activate the versioned `STYLE.PALETTE_VIOLATION` rule against the `verification-report-schema` pipeline (`run_structural_checks`-style library entry point, not the CLI). It must find explicit raw colors across all current style-bearing node properties, including nested nodes; avoid duplicating unknown-role REF findings; use pack-profile severity; emit deterministic Finding fields with actionable role suggestions; and prove at the library level that a fixture with raw colors reports `STYLE.PALETTE_VIOLATION` findings while a role-only fixture passes cleanly and produces an empty violation set. Wiring this rule into the `anima verify` CLI command itself is out of scope here: it belongs to the CLI-integration surface built by `cli-framework` and `verification-report-schema`, which is not yet built in this task's sprint.

verification.command: pytest -q tests/verification/test_palette_rule.py tests/style_packs/test_palette_rule_library_api.py && ruff check src/anima/style_packs src/anima/verification tests/style_packs tests/verification/test_palette_rule.py && mypy --strict src/anima/style_packs src/anima/verification
